passageSets.title: What an Explanation Owes Us
passageSets.passage:
To ask why something is so is to request more than a list of what will happen next; it is to ask that the world be made intelligible. Yet philosophers have long disagreed about what intelligibility requires, and two answers recur. The first holds that an explanation earns its keep by enabling prediction: if a proposed account lets us anticipate what follows, it has done its work, and any further demand is sentiment dressed as rigor. The second holds that an explanation succeeds when it dissolves our perplexity, when the once-puzzling fact comes to feel obvious. Each answer captures something real. Each, I will argue, mistakes a symptom of understanding for its substance, and the cost of the confusion is steep, for it teaches us to accept as explanations things that merely soothe or merely forecast.

Consider first the equation of explanation with prediction. There are countless rules of thumb that forecast splendidly while explaining nothing. A falling barometer reliably heralds a storm, and a sailor who trusts it will rarely be caught unprepared; but the instrument does not explain the weather, for it is itself a downstream sign of the very conditions we wished to understand. To know that one event regularly precedes another is to possess a correlation, and a correlation is a question, not its answer. The predictivist will reply that a sufficiently broad capacity to anticipate just is understanding. But this conflates the traveler who has memorized the timetable with the engineer who grasps why the trains run as they do. The first can tell you when; only the second can tell you why, and can say what would happen were the schedule to change.

The satisfaction view fares no better, though its failure is subtler. The feeling that a fact has become obvious is a notoriously unreliable witness. A confident story, fluently told, can produce that warm click of recognition while smuggling in nothing but familiar words; and a true account, austere and partial, can leave us cold precisely because it refuses the tidy closure we crave. History is littered with explanations that satisfied entire generations and were nonetheless empty, their appeal owing less to insight than to the comfort of fitting a new fact into an old picture. If the measure of an explanation were the relief it brings, the most soothing fable would outrank the most demanding truth. Surely we want the reverse. The feeling of understanding may accompany the real thing, but it can be counterfeited, and a counterfeit that we cannot tell from the genuine article is no standard at all.

What, then, does a good explanation owe us? At minimum, it must locate the puzzling fact within a structure of dependence, showing not only that it holds but on what it rests, so that we could say how things would stand were those grounds altered. Such an account answers the counterfactual questions that mere prediction and mere comfort both ignore. Yet I would add a final, unfashionable demand: honesty about its own reach. An explanation that pretends to exhaust its subject explains less than one that marks the edge of what it has shown. To understand is not to feel finished; it is to see how a fact hangs together with others, and to know, as well, the questions one has only postponed.

hints.prompt: This question asks for the passage's central claim. What is the best approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Identify the author's overall thesis, including what they argue for and what they argue against.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Choose the rival view that the author describes at the greatest length.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pick the first general-sounding statement that appears in the passage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Select one vivid example and treat it as the main point.

hints.rationale: A central-claim question is answered by the author's overall thesis, not by a single example or by a view the author is critiquing.

hints.prompt: How is the author's argument organized, which tells you where the central claim lies?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A list of unrelated facts with no argument at all.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two rival criteria are each shown to be inadequate, and then the author offers his own account of what an explanation requires.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A single story told mainly to entertain the reader.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A neutral survey that endorses every view it mentions.

hints.rationale: Recognizing the 'reject two rivals, then offer my own standard' structure points you to the author's positive thesis as the central claim.

hints.prompt: One tempting answer says an explanation is adequate only if it lets us reliably predict future events. Why is that a trap?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because the author accepts prediction as fully sufficient for explanation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the barometer example shows something can predict a storm without explaining it, so prediction alone is not enough.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because the passage never discusses prediction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because the passage claims prediction is impossible.

hints.rationale: Prediction-only is exactly the view the barometer refutes, so that choice restates a position the author rejects.

questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the central claim of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An explanation is adequate only if it allows us to predict future events reliably.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A good explanation must situate a fact within a structure of dependence and remain honest about its limits, rather than merely enabling prediction or producing a feeling of satisfaction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because both prediction and satisfaction can be counterfeited, no account deserves to be called a genuine explanation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The feeling that a puzzle has been solved is the surest sign that a real explanation has been found.

questions.explanation: B is correct because it captures the author's positive thesis, that explanation locates a fact in a structure of dependence while remaining honest about its limits, and it rejects the two rival criteria. A is wrong because it states the predictivist view the author refutes with the barometer and timetable examples. C is wrong because it overstates the case, since the author holds that good explanations do exist, namely those that answer counterfactual questions. D is wrong because it endorses the satisfaction view the author criticizes as prone to counterfeits.

hints.prompt: This question asks about the author's attitude toward the two rival views. What should the answer be based on?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The author's evaluative word choice and how he judges the views.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The overall subject matter the passage happens to cover.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the two views are, in fact, scientifically correct.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The single most emotional word found anywhere in the passage.

hints.rationale: Attitude questions are answered from the author's evaluative stance shown through word choice, not from the topic alone.

hints.prompt: Which sentence is the strongest clue to the author's attitude toward the two views?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'To ask why something is so is to request more than a list of what will happen next.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Each answer captures something real. Each, I will argue, mistakes a symptom of understanding for its substance.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'A falling barometer reliably heralds a storm.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'What, then, does a good explanation owe us?'

hints.rationale: That sentence both credits the views ('captures something real') and criticizes them, making it the key clue to the author's mixed attitude.

hints.prompt: A tempting wrong answer calls the tone 'open contempt for those who hold the views.' Why is that incorrect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because the author actually agrees with both views.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the author criticizes the views analytically and even grants that each captures something real, which is inconsistent with contempt.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because the author never criticizes the views in any way.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because the passage is written only to entertain.

hints.rationale: Measured criticism paired with genuine concessions rules out contempt as a description of the tone.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the prediction-based and satisfaction-based views is best described as

choices.label: A
choices.text: open contempt for those who hold them

choices.label: B
choices.text: complete neutrality, presenting them without judgment

choices.label: C
choices.text: enthusiastic endorsement of both as fully adequate

choices.label: D
choices.text: critical but respectful, granting that each captures something real before arguing that each mistakes a symptom for the substance

questions.explanation: D is correct because the author concedes that each view captures something real yet judges each inadequate, a measured and respectful criticism. A is wrong because the tone is analytical rather than contemptuous. B is wrong because the author plainly evaluates and rejects both as criteria, so he is not neutral. C is wrong because he does not endorse either view; he treats their insights as symptoms, not standards.

hints.prompt: This question asks what the barometer example is doing in the argument. What kind of task is that?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A function question: ask what job the example does for the author's larger point.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A question about whether barometers actually work.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A request to summarize the whole passage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A question about whether the example is well written.

hints.rationale: 'Serves to' signals a function question, so you must ask what role the detail plays, not whether it is true.

hints.prompt: What point is the barometer paired with in the same paragraph?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That instruments keep improving our understanding of nature.

choices.label: B
choices.text: That a correlation is 'a question, not its answer,' so tracking an event differs from explaining it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: That storms are dangerous to sailors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: That prediction requires a prior explanation.

hints.rationale: The barometer sits beside the claim that a correlation is not an explanation, which fixes its purpose in the argument.

hints.prompt: A tempting wrong answer says the barometer shows 'prediction is impossible without first having an explanation.' Why is that a trap?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It reverses the point; the barometer predicts well yet explains nothing, so prediction plainly does not require prior explanation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the author says prediction is worthless.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because barometers do not really predict storms.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because the example is about satisfaction, not prediction.

hints.rationale: The example separates successful prediction from explanation, the opposite of saying prediction needs an explanation first.

questions.stem: The example of the falling barometer that heralds a storm primarily serves to

choices.label: A
choices.text: show that something can reliably predict an event without explaining it

choices.label: B
choices.text: prove that prediction is impossible without first having an explanation

choices.label: C
choices.text: illustrate how instruments steadily improve our understanding of nature

choices.label: D
choices.text: demonstrate that correlations are usually mistaken

questions.explanation: A is correct because the barometer predicts the storm yet does not explain the weather, separating prediction from explanation. B is wrong because it reverses and overstates the point; the author does not claim prediction requires prior explanation. C is wrong because it reads the example as praising instruments and understanding, the opposite of its use. D is wrong because the author says a correlation is a question rather than an answer, not that correlations are typically false.

hints.prompt: This question asks what the author would infer about an austere truth that 'leaves us cold.' What approach fits?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Combine the author's stated principles to reach a conclusion he implies but does not state outright.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Find a sentence that answers the question word for word.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pick the most emotionally comforting option.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Choose an answer that contradicts the author's stance.

hints.rationale: Inference questions require extending the author's principles, not locating a verbatim restatement.

hints.prompt: Which sentence is the key clue for this inference?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'A falling barometer reliably heralds a storm.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'The feeling of understanding may accompany the real thing, but it can be counterfeited, and a counterfeit that we cannot tell from the genuine article is no standard at all.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'To ask why something is so is to request more than a list of what will happen next.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'the engineer who grasps why the trains run as they do.'

hints.rationale: The sentence about the feeling of understanding being a counterfeit is what licenses any inference about an austere but true account.

hints.prompt: Two answer choices judge an explanation by whether it 'feels obvious' or brings 'relief.' Why can both be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because they rely on the satisfaction standard the author explicitly rejects as unreliable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the author says only comforting explanations are true.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because the author never mentions feelings at all.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because they are about prediction, which the author endorses.

hints.rationale: Any choice that makes the feeling of relief or obviousness the criterion adopts the very view the passage argues against.

questions.stem: It can be inferred that the author would regard a 'true account, austere and partial' that leaves readers cold as

choices.label: A
choices.text: inferior to a satisfying fable, since explanations should bring relief

choices.label: B
choices.text: a failure, because an explanation that does not feel obvious has not done its job

choices.label: C
choices.text: potentially a better explanation than a soothing story, since the feeling of understanding is an unreliable measure

choices.label: D
choices.text: identical in value to any account that predicts equally well

questions.explanation: C is correct because the author argues that satisfaction can be counterfeited, so an austere truth may explain better than a comforting fable. A is wrong because it endorses the satisfaction standard the author rejects, treating relief as the test. B is wrong because it likewise makes the feeling of obviousness the criterion, which the passage denies. D is wrong because it imports the predictivist criterion, which the passage also rejects and which is irrelevant to the contrast being drawn.

hints.prompt: This question asks which scenario best fits the kind of understanding the author endorses. What must you do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply the author's own standard for explanation to each new case and see which one satisfies it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pick whichever scenario is mentioned in the passage itself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Choose any scenario that involves learning of some kind.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Select the scenario that best illustrates mere prediction.

hints.rationale: Application questions require matching a new situation to the author's principle, not restating an example.

hints.prompt: What is the author's positive standard for genuine understanding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reliably predicting what will happen next.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Locating a fact within a structure of dependence and being able to say how things would differ if the grounds changed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Producing a warm feeling that a puzzle is solved.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Memorizing a regularity so it can be anticipated.

hints.rationale: The correct scenario must show dependence and answer counterfactual ('what would change') questions, the author's stated criterion.

hints.prompt: A tempting wrong answer describes a forecaster who anticipates outcomes accurately but cannot say why. Why is it a trap?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because it describes exactly the mere-prediction case the author says is not real understanding.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because forecasting is impossible in principle.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because the author praises accurate forecasting above all.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because the scenario involves no people.

hints.rationale: A predictor who cannot explain 'why' embodies the prediction-without-understanding the author rejects.

questions.stem: Which scenario best exemplifies the kind of understanding the author endorses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A student memorizes that a certain ritual is always followed by a holiday and can predict the holiday's arrival.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A historian shows how a war depended on specific prior tensions and can say how events would have differed had those tensions been absent.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A reader feels a deep sense of closure upon hearing a confident, fluent account of an event.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A forecaster anticipates outcomes accurately but cannot say why the pattern holds or what would change it.

questions.explanation: B is correct because it embodies the author's positive standard: locating a fact in a structure of dependence and answering counterfactual questions. A is wrong because it illustrates mere prediction by a memorizer who tracks a regularity without grasping why. C is wrong because it illustrates the counterfeit feeling of understanding the author warns against. D is wrong because the forecaster predicts without explaining, exactly what the author says is not understanding.

hints.prompt: This question asks for an assumption the argument depends on. What are you looking for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An unstated premise the argument needs in order to hold together.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A restatement of the argument's explicit conclusion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A claim the author openly denies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A true but irrelevant statement.

hints.rationale: Assumption questions ask for the hidden premise the reasoning requires, not the conclusion or an off-topic fact.

hints.prompt: Which claim is the one whose hidden premise you must find?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The author's claim that the feeling of understanding 'can be counterfeited' and so is 'no standard at all.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: The claim that prediction always requires a prior explanation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The claim that comforting fables are always factually false.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The claim that every explanation must be complete.

hints.rationale: Pinning down the exact claim about a 'counterfeit' feeling is the step before asking what that claim must quietly presuppose.

hints.prompt: A tempting wrong answer is 'a complete explanation must leave no question unanswered.' Why can it be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because it contradicts the passage, which praises explanations that honestly mark the edge of what they have shown.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the author never discusses complete explanations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because it restates the barometer example.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because the author says explanations should bring comfort.

hints.rationale: The author values honesty about limits, so requiring that nothing be left unanswered runs against the passage rather than being an assumption of it.

questions.stem: The author's argument depends on which of the following assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prediction and explanation never overlap in practice.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Most people prefer comforting falsehoods to demanding truths.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A complete explanation must leave no question unanswered.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is possible to distinguish genuine understanding from a mere feeling of understanding.

questions.explanation: D is correct because the claim that the feeling of understanding can be counterfeited only has force if genuine understanding can be told apart from that feeling. A is wrong because it is not required; the author concedes a good explanation may also predict, denying only that prediction alone suffices. B is wrong because it is a psychological claim the argument never needs. C is wrong because it contradicts the passage, which praises explanations that mark the edge of what they have shown.

passageSets.title: Reading the Old Words
passageSets.passage:
Few disputes in legal theory are staged as melodramatically as the quarrel between those who would fix a constitution's meaning at the moment of its framing and those who would let that meaning grow with the society it governs. The originalist insists that words enacted long ago mean now what they meant then, and that to hold otherwise is to let judges rewrite the law under cover of reading it. The proponent of a living document replies that a charter meant to endure for centuries cannot be chained to the assumptions of a single generation, and that fidelity to enduring principles sometimes requires departing from their authors' narrower expectations. Each side accuses the other of the same sin, the smuggling of personal preference into the guise of interpretation, and each is convinced that only the rival doctrine makes such smuggling possible.

Yet the originalist's promise of mechanical objectivity dissolves on contact with the texts it venerates. Old words are often general words, and a general word does not announce the level of generality at which it should be read. When a charter forbids cruel punishments, does it forbid only those practices its authors would have named as cruel, or does it enact a principle against cruelty whose applications they could not foresee? Nothing in the bare words decides this, and the originalist must choose; but the choice is precisely the interpretive act that the doctrine claimed to render unnecessary. To pick the narrow reading is no more neutral than to pick the broad one, for both are answers to a question the text leaves open. The pretense that history hands down a single determinate meaning, requiring only transcription, mistakes the difficulty of the work for its absence.

This is not, however, a vindication of the opposing camp. If the objection to originalism is that it hides a choice, the objection to its rival is that it may license too many. Once meaning is permitted to evolve, something must constrain the direction of its growth, lest the document become a mirror in which each interpreter sees only the reforms he already favored. An appeal to evolving standards is empty unless it can say whose standards, measured how, and why those rather than others; otherwise the living document lives only in the sense that it does whatever its current readers wish. The honest living-constitutionalist therefore owes us an account of discipline as surely as the originalist owes us an account of choice. Neither can claim the high ground of pure constraint, because both are doing the same difficult thing under different names.

The productive question, then, is not whether meaning is fixed or fluid, but who must justify what, and how openly. A mature jurisprudence treats the text as setting presumptions rather than issuing commands that read themselves: its language establishes where the burden of argument falls, so that one who would depart from the most natural reading must say why, in the open, on grounds others can examine. What corrupts interpretation is not change but concealment, the dressing of a contestable choice as the discovery of an obvious meaning. Both doctrines, in their dogmatic forms, encourage that disguise. Stripped of their pretensions, each becomes a reminder to the other: that words constrain, and that words alone never decide. Wisdom lies not in choosing a slogan but in keeping the work of choosing visible.

hints.prompt: This is a main-idea question about a debate. What is the best approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Identify the author's overall synthesis, not either side's slogan.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Choose whichever doctrine the author names first.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pick the doctrine described in the most vivid language.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Treat a single example as the entire point.

hints.rationale: A main-idea question about a debate wants the author's own synthesis, not a partisan slogan or one example.

hints.prompt: What does the author conclude about the originalist's 'promise of mechanical objectivity'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is fully delivered once we consult original meaning.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It dissolves because general words do not announce the level of generality at which they should be read.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is irrelevant to how judges actually rule.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It proves the living-document view entirely correct.

hints.rationale: Showing that originalism cannot be purely mechanical is the pivotal step toward the author's reframing of the debate.

hints.prompt: A tempting wrong answer says interpretation should be governed entirely by the original authors' stated expectations. Why is it a trap?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because the author shows the words often leave the level of generality open, so pure originalism cannot escape interpretive choices.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the author says original meaning is completely irrelevant.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because the passage concerns only statutes, never constitutions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because the author endorses whatever today's readers prefer.

hints.rationale: The passage argues that original expectations cannot settle meaning by themselves, so that choice adopts a view the author undercuts.

questions.stem: The main point of the passage is that

choices.label: A
choices.text: originalism is clearly superior because it ties meaning to the moment of enactment

choices.label: B
choices.text: the living-document view is correct because constitutions must adapt to new circumstances

choices.label: C
choices.text: the opposition between the two doctrines is overdrawn, since both require interpretive choices, and the real issue is who must justify departures and how openly

choices.label: D
choices.text: constitutional interpretation should be governed entirely by the original authors' stated expectations

questions.explanation: C is correct because it states the author's synthesis: both camps interpret, and the productive question concerns the burden of justification and transparency. A is wrong because the author shows originalism cannot deliver the mechanical objectivity it promises. B is wrong because the author faults the living-document view for failing to supply needed discipline. D is wrong because it endorses an extreme originalism the passage explicitly undermines.

choices.label: A

choices.label: B
choices.text: A summary of what each doctrine claims.

choices.label: C

choices.label: D
choices.text: The single most forceful word anywhere in the passage.

hints.rationale: Attitude questions turn on the author's evaluative stance, revealed through word choice, not on the doctrines' content.

hints.prompt: Which phrase most directly signals the author's view of the two doctrines 'in their dogmatic forms'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Few disputes in legal theory are staged as melodramatically...'

choices.label: B
choices.text: Each doctrine, 'stripped of their pretensions,' becomes 'a reminder to the other' that words constrain and that words alone never decide.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Old words are often general words.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'a charter forbids cruel punishments.'

hints.prompt: A tempting wrong answer calls the author 'wholly dismissive of both doctrines as worthless.' Why is that incorrect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because the author says each doctrine, once chastened, still usefully reminds the other of a real truth, so he does not treat them as worthless.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the author fully endorses both doctrines as they stand.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because the author discusses only one of the two doctrines.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because the author refuses to evaluate either doctrine.

hints.rationale: Finding residual value in each doctrine rules out calling them worthless.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward both doctrines in their 'dogmatic forms' is best characterized as

choices.label: A
choices.text: skeptical of each one's pretensions while finding value in each when chastened

choices.label: B
choices.text: wholly dismissive of both as worthless

choices.label: C
choices.text: admiring of originalism and hostile to its rival

choices.label: D
choices.text: indifferent, since he regards the debate as unimportant

questions.explanation: A is correct because the author punctures each doctrine's pretensions yet says each, stripped of them, usefully reminds the other of a truth. B is wrong because he finds value in both once chastened, not worthlessness. C is wrong because he criticizes both rather than favoring one. D is wrong because he treats the debate as important enough to reframe rather than dismiss.

passageSets.title: The Reach of Moral Concern
passageSets.passage:
When we ask which creatures matter morally, we are really asking what features of a being make its fate a claim upon us rather than a matter of indifference. For much of the Western tradition the answer was rationality, or language, or the capacity to enter into agreements, and since other animals were thought to lack these, they fell outside the circle of direct concern. We might be forbidden to torment a dog, but only because cruelty coarsens the person who indulges it, not because the dog itself was wronged. I want to ask why intelligence should be the price of admission. A being need not follow an argument or compose a sentence in order to have a stake in how its life goes; it needs only to be the kind of thing for which its life can go well or badly from the inside.

The feature that does the work, I suggest, is sentience: the capacity to suffer and to enjoy, to have experiences that are better or worse for the one who has them. A creature that can feel pain is not merely a complicated object; there is something it is like to be it, and its pain is bad for it in the only sense that finally matters, namely from within its own experience. Once we see this, the boundary of species looks suspiciously like other boundaries we have learned to distrust. To count a being's suffering for less simply because it belongs to another kind is structurally akin to discounting the suffering of those outside one's tribe: in each case a morally irrelevant category is asked to do the work that only the reality of suffering can do. The pain of a creature very unlike us is still pain, and it does not hurt any less for being felt by a stranger.

To grant that sentience matters is not to claim that every interest weighs the same. Equal consideration of interests is not identical treatment, and beings differ in what they can lose. A creature that cannot conceive of its own future may be harmed by present pain yet not by the mere fact of an earlier death, since it has no projects cut short and no anticipated years foreclosed. Such differences are real and may justify treating cases differently. What they cannot justify is ignoring an interest altogether. The mistake of the tradition was not that it weighed human lives heavily, but that it weighed animal suffering at nothing, treating a genuine claim as if it were no claim at all. Sentience marks a threshold of moral considerability; above that threshold, careful comparison begins. Below it, there is nothing to consider.

If this is right, then the most striking feature of our common practices is how thoroughly they proceed as though the threshold did not exist. We raise and kill sentient creatures on a vast scale for reasons of taste and convenience, and we rarely pause to ask whether those reasons are weighty enough to override the interests at stake. I do not claim that every such practice is indefensible; I claim that each requires a defense, and that the burden has been resting on the wrong side. Once we admit that suffering counts wherever it is felt, the question is no longer whether animals may be considered, but how much our habits would have to change if we took the answer seriously. That is an uncomfortable question, which is perhaps why it is so seldom asked.

passageSets.title: What Survives a Retelling
passageSets.passage:
There is a familiar lament that myths, as they pass from one tongue and one century to another, are progressively corrupted, so that what reaches us is a faded copy of some lost and luminous original. On this view the task of the scholar is forensic: to strip away the accretions of careless retelling and recover the authentic tale as it was first told. The lament is understandable, and not wholly wrong, but it rests on an assumption worth questioning, namely that a myth is the kind of thing that has an original in the first place. A poem has an author and a text; we can ask what its words are and whether a translation has betrayed them. A myth, by contrast, arrives already multiple, told in countless versions none of which can claim to be the first, and it is precisely this multiplicity, I will argue, that keeps it alive.

What, then, survives when a myth crosses a border? Not, for the most part, its furniture: the names of its gods, the local rivers, the particular beasts are the first things to change, and they change without injury. What persists is something more like a shape of concern, a question the story exists to pose and to hold open. A tale of descent into a land of the dead may swap every proper noun and still remain, recognizably, an attempt to think about loss and the longing to undo it. The retellers are not failing to copy; they are doing what the myth is for, bending an inherited shape to a new community's fears. Even mistranslation, so often mourned, can be generative, for a word misheard may open a meaning the careful scholar would have closed. The history of a myth is the history of its uses, and a thing that is used is a thing that lives.

None of this licenses the comforting notion that any retelling is as good as any other. To say that a myth lives through its versions is not to say that all versions serve it equally. A retelling can flatten its source, trading a hard question for an easy reassurance, resolving in a sentence what the older tale had the courage to leave unresolved. When a story of dangerous knowledge becomes a tidy fable with a moral stamped on the last page, something has indeed been lost, not because a detail was altered but because the concern was domesticated. The right standard, then, is not fidelity to wording but fidelity to difficulty: does the new version keep open the question the old one posed, or does it sell that question for a cheap and final answer? Judged this way, the most literal translation can be the greater betrayal.

To worry, then, that retelling endangers a myth is to misunderstand the kind of thing a myth is. The poem we can lose, for it is its words; the myth we keep precisely by remaking it, and the version that never changed would be the one that had died, embalmed in a single tongue and a single age. What we inherit is not a relic to be guarded but an invitation to be answered, and we answer it whether we mean to or not, every time we tell the old stories to a new listener. The scholar who mourns the vanished original mourns a thing that never was. The rest of us are simply its newest authors, adding our versions to a conversation that has no first word and, with luck, no last.

passageSets.title: Whose Neighborhood Improves
passageSets.passage:
Few urban phenomena are argued about with as much heat and as little precision as gentrification. To its boosters, the word names a welcome revival: capital returns to long-neglected districts, shuttered storefronts reopen, public services improve, and a neighborhood that was written off acquires cafes, safety, and a tax base. To its critics, the same word names an injury: long-standing residents are priced out, the textures of a community are sanded smooth for newcomers, and a place loses the people who made it worth moving to. Each camp speaks as though gentrification were a single thing, to be embraced or resisted whole. I want to suggest that this framing is the source of the confusion, because it bundles together processes that can, and should, be pulled apart. The interesting question is not whether neighborhoods should change, which they always have, but who decides, who pays, and who keeps the gains.

Begin by separating reinvestment from displacement. There is nothing intrinsically harmful in a neighborhood gaining better transit, cleaner parks, or a grocery that sells fresh food; these are goods that residents of poor districts have long been denied, and to oppose them in the name of authenticity is to condemn the poor to permanent neglect for the comfort of observers who do not live there. The romance of the unchanged neighborhood is usually written by people with the option to leave. What turns improvement into injury is displacement: the process by which those who endured a place at its worst are unable to remain for its recovery. Rising rents, converted buildings, and the quiet pressure on those who no longer fit the new image do the work. Improvement and displacement often travel together, but they are not the same thing, and conflating them disarms us.

Once the two are distinguished, a sharper question comes into view: who captures the value that change creates? A neighborhood's revival is rarely the achievement of the newcomers who profit from it. It is produced, in large part, by public investment in transit and schools, and by the slow, unpaid labor of residents who tended the place through its hard years, building the very networks and character that later make it desirable. Yet when the value of all this finally appears, it is realized as higher rents and rising property values that flow to landlords and speculators, while the people whose presence helped create the appeal receive an eviction notice. The grievance, properly stated, is not that a neighborhood improved. It is that the improvement was, in effect, financed by the public and the poor and harvested by the few. That is a distributional outrage, not an aesthetic one.

Framing the matter this way changes what a solution would look like. If the problem were change as such, the only remedy would be to freeze neighborhoods in place, a cure that mostly preserves disadvantage. But if the problem is who keeps the gains, then the remedy is to alter that distribution: to give long-time residents a durable claim on the value their presence helped create, so that they may stay to enjoy the improvement rather than subsidize someone else's profit from afar. The details of how are contestable and genuinely hard. The point here is prior to those details. So long as we argue only about whether neighborhoods should change, we will keep missing the question that matters, and the answer that the unromantic facts keep pressing upon us: change for whom, and at whose expense?

passageSets.title: A Divided Inheritance
passageSets.passage:
The Enlightenment is less a period than a battleground over which two opposed stories are endlessly fought. In the first, it is the dawn from which everything good in modern life descends: the dignity of the individual, the authority of evidence over superstition, the idea that institutions must answer to reason rather than to inherited privilege. In the second, it is the source of our characteristic arrogance: a cold faith in calculation, a contempt for tradition and for ways of knowing it could not measure, and a universalism that, claiming to speak for all humanity, too often spoke for a narrow part of it while subjugating the rest. Each story can marshal an impressive array of evidence, and each, told alone, is a kind of lie. What strikes me is how rarely the partisans notice that they are quarreling about the same inheritance, as if it had to be all gift or all curse.

The truth, harder to make into a slogan, is that the inheritance is divided against itself. The same impulse that taught people to demand reasons for their subjection also taught a confidence that the world, once measured, could be mastered, and mastery has never restricted itself to rivers and harvests. The insistence that no authority stands above criticism armed the movements that abolished old cruelties; the conviction that whatever could not be counted hardly counted at all licensed new ones. These are not two unrelated currents that happen to share a name. They flow from a single source, the determination to submit every inherited thing to the tribunal of reason, which is at once the most liberating and the most dangerous idea the age produced. To praise the one current while disowning the other is to pretend that a tool can be designed to cut in only the directions we approve.

If the legacy is genuinely double, then neither celebration nor repudiation will do, and a third stance is required, one suggested by the age itself. For the deepest principle of the Enlightenment was not any particular doctrine but a method: the refusal to exempt anything from criticism. Applied consistently, that method must turn upon the movement that gave it birth, exposing the parochial interests that masqueraded as universal truths and the dogmas that reason smuggled in under its own banner. This is not a betrayal of the Enlightenment but its most faithful continuation. Notice, too, that the fashionable wholesale rejection of reason cannot help borrowing reason's tools, since it argues, marshals evidence, and asks us to be persuaded. One cannot reason one's way out of reasoning. The honest path is not to abandon the inheritance but to hold it to its own demanding standard.

To inherit the Enlightenment maturely, then, is to give up the comfort of a verdict. We are not asked to decide whether the age was, on balance, good or bad, as though history were a ledger awaiting a final sum. We are asked to do the slower work of sorting: to carry forward the demand for reasons and the dignity of the individual while disarming the arrogance that so often accompanied them. That work is unglamorous and never finished, which is why the polemicists on both sides prefer their tidy verdicts. But an inheritance is not a monument to be defended or toppled; it is a set of tools and temptations handed to the living, who must decide, again in each generation, which to use and which to set down.

passageSets.title: The Memory in a Meal
passageSets.passage:
To ask what a people eat is never merely to ask how they obtain their calories. A cuisine is among the most durable forms a culture takes, carried in the body long after languages are forgotten and homelands left behind. The migrant who can no longer name the streets of a childhood city can still reproduce, from the hands and not from any book, the precise sequence by which a grandmother folded dough or seasoned a stew, and in the doing summons a whole vanished world. Taste and smell, more than sight or sound, seem to keep their hold on us, so that a single mouthful can return a person to a kitchen decades gone. But the power of food is not only private. Shared meals mark who belongs and who is guest; the dishes a community insists upon at its festivals are declarations of who its members take themselves to be.

And yet the moment a cuisine is made into a banner, a familiar error follows: the demand for authenticity, the belief that there exists a pure and original version from which all later cooking is a falling away. The anthropologist of food learns to treat this belief with affectionate skepticism. Trace almost any beloved national dish and you find a history of borrowing: ingredients that arrived by trade or conquest only a few generations ago, techniques learned from neighbors and rivals, peppers and tomatoes and grains that crossed oceans to reach the kitchens that now treat them as immemorial. The cuisine a people defends as timeless is usually a snapshot of a particular, recent moment in a long process of exchange. To hunt for the authentic original is to misunderstand what a cuisine is: not a relic preserved, but a conversation that never stopped.

Here a tension seems to open: if cuisines are forever changing, how can they carry identity across generations at all? The resolution lies in seeing where the continuity actually resides. It is not in any fixed list of ingredients but in practice and in meaning, in the act of cooking and eating together and in the place a dish holds within a shared life. A family's holiday table may, over a century, swap nearly every item upon it, and yet the gathering, the labor of preparation, and the sense that this meal is the one that must be made remain unbroken. What is transmitted is less a recipe than a relationship to a recipe, a way of caring that a particular food matters. Identity rides not on the substance of the dish but on its role, which is why a cuisine can change completely and still be felt, rightly, as the same.

This suggests a different way to honor a cuisine than the anxious guarding of authenticity. The diaspora cook who improvises with what the new country offers, substituting one green for another and inventing as necessity demands, is not corrupting a tradition but doing exactly what every generation before did. The purist who freezes a dish into a single sanctioned form, by contrast, has mistaken a photograph for a life. To keep a cuisine is to keep cooking it, which means to keep changing it, because that is the only way anything living has ever been kept. The memory a food carries is real, and the identity it sustains is no illusion. But both live in the doing, passed from hand to hand, and not in any pure original that, on inspection, turns out never to have existed.

passageSets.title: Consenting to Be Small
passageSets.passage:
The literature of wild places has always reached for a feeling that ordinary words for pleasure cannot hold. We call a meadow or a melody beautiful, and we mean that it pleases us, that it sits comfortably within our powers of taking in. But the great nature writers reach instead for the sublime, and they mean something almost opposite: the vast, the formless, the overwhelming, the storm-darkened range or the indifferent ocean that does not please us so much as stagger us. Beauty flatters the perceiver; the sublime dwarfs him. Confronted with a magnitude that exceeds any human scale, the mind is first overwhelmed, made to feel its own smallness, and only then, in a second movement, finds something exhilarating in the very fact that it can stand before such immensity and know it for what it is. This double motion, humbling and then strangely elevating, is what the word has long tried to name.

Why should such an unsettling feeling be prized? Because, I want to argue, its chief gift is the humbling itself. We move through our days as the unquestioned centers of our own concerns, measuring the world by its usefulness to us, and this habit, comfortable as it is, narrows the soul. The sublime interrupts it. To stand beneath a sky indifferent to whether one lives or dies is to be reminded, forcibly and for once, that one is not the measure of all things, that the world vastly exceeds the small theater of human purposes. There is a kind of health in this. The encounter does not merely frighten; it corrects a distortion, restoring a truer sense of proportion between the self and everything that is not the self. The nature writers at their best are not describing scenery. They are administering a tonic against our chronic self-importance.

This is precisely why the contemporary appetite for the sublime so often betrays it. We have learned to pursue awe as an experience to be acquired, a summit to be reached and recorded, a view to be captured and displayed as evidence of a life well spent. But notice what this does. The moment the encounter becomes a possession of mine, a trophy in the museum of my experiences, the self that the sublime was meant to dethrone has clambered back onto its throne, larger than before. The mountain that should have humbled me becomes a backdrop for my image; the indifferent ocean is enlisted to flatter the very ego it ought to have rebuked. Awe pursued as personal enhancement is no longer awe; it is self-regard in borrowed grandeur. The thing most worth seeking is, by a cruel logic, the thing that the wrong kind of seeking reliably destroys.

What, then, would it mean to seek the sublime rightly? Perhaps the paradox is that it cannot be sought at all in the acquisitive sense, only prepared for and then received. It asks not that we add an experience to our collection but that we consent, for a moment, to be small, to let the self fall silent before something that has no interest in us whatever. This is not a technique that can be optimized, and it yields no trophy to display. Its only reward is the brief and bracing correction of our proportions, the reminder that we are a small part of a vast and self-sufficient world. The nature writers who still move us are the ones who knew this, and who tried, against the grain of every acquisitive habit, to teach us how to be diminished, and to be grateful for it.

passageSets.title: Two Gods or One Quarrel
passageSets.passage:
It is a commonplace of political argument that liberty and equality are rival gods, and that to serve one is necessarily to slight the other. On this view, every gain in equality is purchased by a loss of freedom, since making people more equal requires constraining what the fortunate may do, and every expansion of liberty must widen the gaps between persons, since free people will use their freedom unequally and pull apart. The libertarian therefore guards liberty and accepts the inequalities that follow as the price of freedom; the egalitarian presses for equality and accepts the coercion required as the price of fairness. Each regards the other's ideal as a standing threat to his own. I do not deny that the values can collide. But I want to argue that the familiar picture of a simple trade-off, more of one bought only with less of the other, badly distorts the relation between them.

The distortion begins with an ambiguity in the word liberty. To be free may mean merely that no law forbids one to act, or it may mean that one actually has the capacity to act. The two come apart sharply. A right to an education that one is too poor to obtain, a freedom to travel that one can never afford, a liberty to publish for those who own no press: such freedoms are formally real and practically hollow, and to celebrate them while ignoring the conditions for using them is to mistake a permission for a power. Once we attend to effective liberty, the supposed opposition softens, for many of the equalities the egalitarian seeks are not constraints upon freedom but the very means by which freedom is made real for those who would otherwise possess it in name alone. A society of formal liberties enjoyed by few is not a free society generously shared.

I do not pretend this dissolves every conflict. Some measures taken in the name of equality genuinely do restrict genuine liberties: to redistribute is to constrain what the prosperous may keep and do, and it is dishonest to deny that something is thereby lost. The point is not that liberty and equality never collide but that they do not collide as wholes, two armies meeting on an open field. They collide at particular points, over particular liberties and particular equalities, and these specific conflicts must be judged on their specific merits. Is the liberty being curtailed a weighty one or a trivial one? Is the equality being purchased a precondition of others' effective freedom or a mere leveling for its own sake? These are answerable questions. The slogan that pits Liberty against Equality, each capitalized and entire, makes them unanswerable by hiding the only details that matter.

The mature question, then, is never whether to choose liberty or equality, as if one had to bow to a single idol and smash the other. It is always which liberties, secured for whom, and which equalities, purchased at what cost to which freedoms. Posed that way, the issue returns from the heaven of abstractions to the ground of judgment, where it belongs. There it turns out that the two ideals are entangled far more than they are opposed, each in part depending on the other, and that the honest disagreements among us are seldom between the friends of freedom and the friends of fairness. They are between people who weigh particular liberties and particular equalities differently, which is a quarrel worth having, and a very different thing from a war between two gods.

passageSets.title: The Unsorted Present
passageSets.passage:
Almost everyone, at some point, feels it: the sense that life has begun to move too fast, that the present rushes by before it can be grasped, and that somewhere behind us lies a slower, more coherent age we have lost. Two responses to this feeling are ready to hand, and both are too quick. The first is the debunker's: every generation has complained of acceleration and mourned a vanished calm, and since the complaint is perennial it must be an illusion, a mere trick of memory. The second is the alarmist's: this time the feeling is simply true, the pace really has become inhuman, and our unease is an accurate report of a degraded world. I find neither satisfying. To treat the feeling as pure error is to ignore how widely and intensely it is felt; to treat it as pure perception is to ignore how often it has misfired before.

A better approach treats the feeling not as a claim to be verified or refuted but as a symptom to be interpreted. What condition, we should ask, would produce so persistent and so widely shared a sense of acceleration? The answer, I suggest, has less to do with the literal speed of events than with our capacity to absorb them. We make sense of our lives by weaving experience into a continuous story, fitting each new thing into a remembered pattern. Nostalgia arises when that work falls behind, when the present delivers more change than memory can metabolize into a coherent narrative. The past feels slower not because it was, but because it is finished: its events have already been sorted, assigned their places, and made to cohere. The present feels frantic because it is still unsorted, arriving faster than we can tell ourselves what it means.

Technology enters this account not as a villain but as an accelerant of a particular kind. Its effect is less to speed up events than to multiply discontinuities, to flood us with the new at the expense of the repeated. The rhythms by which earlier lives were metabolized, the recurring seasons of work, the rituals that returned on schedule, the slow ripening of a reputation or a craft, were engines of narrative continuity: they supplied the repetition against which change becomes legible. A device that offers something unprecedented every hour erodes exactly this. It does not merely fill our time; it fragments the background of sameness that once let us notice, and absorb, what had genuinely changed. The result is a present experienced as a blur of novelty without pattern, which is precisely the condition under which the sense of unbearable acceleration, and the longing for a coherent past, is most likely to take hold.

If this is right, the feeling deserves neither the debunker's dismissal nor the alarmist's surrender. It is information, a signal that a genuine need is going unmet, the need to make experience cohere. The longing for a slower past is, decoded, a longing for the conditions under which change can be absorbed rather than merely endured. That reframing matters, because it changes what we might do. We cannot return to a vanished age, and we would not like it if we could. But we can ask, together, how to rebuild the repetitions and rituals that let a life be narrated, how to win back the background of sameness against which the new becomes meaningful. The accelerating present is not a fate to be mourned or denied. It is a problem of metabolism, and problems of metabolism, unlike the passage of time, can sometimes be addressed.

choices.text: argue that the present is objectively faster than the past

choices.label: D
choices.text: explain why the past seems calmer, namely that its events have already been sorted into a coherent order, unlike the unsorted present

questions.explanation: D is correct because the claim explains the contrast in feeling by the past's events being already sorted while the present is not. A is wrong because it misreads the claim as asserting the past truly was slower. B is wrong because it credits memory with an accuracy the author is questioning. C is wrong because it imports an objective-speed claim the author declines to make.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the author's claim that technology heightens the sense of acceleration by multiplying discontinuities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: People today sleep roughly as many hours as people did a century ago.

choices.label: B
choices.text: People whose days are filled with constant novel inputs report a weaker sense of routine and a stronger feeling that time is rushing by.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Older technologies were also once criticized as too fast.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Most new devices are more energy-efficient than older ones.

A is wrong because sleep duration is irrelevant to narrative continuity. C is wrong because it supports the debunker's perennial-complaint point rather than the author's mechanism. D is wrong because efficiency is unrelated to the experience of acceleration.

questions.stem: The author's argument relies on which of the following assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: People make sense of their experience by fitting it into a continuous narrative or pattern.

choices.label: B

choices.label: C

choices.label: D

questions.explanation: A is correct because the metabolism account rests on the premise that we understand life by weaving it into a continuous story. B is wrong because the author deliberately sets that aside, locating the issue in absorption rather than literal speed. C is wrong because the author denies it, treating the past's calm as partly an artifact of sorting. D is wrong because the author calls technology an accelerant, not a pure evil.

questions.stem: In context, the phrase that the present arrives 'faster than we can tell ourselves what it means' most nearly conveys that

choices.label: A
choices.text: events literally occur at a higher physical speed than before

choices.label: B
choices.text: people have stopped trying to understand their lives at all

choices.label: C
choices.text: change is outpacing our ability to interpret it and fit it into a coherent story

choices.label: D
choices.text: the future is impossible to predict with any accuracy

questions.explanation: C is correct because the phrase says interpretation lags behind the rate of change, leaving the present unsorted. A is wrong because it takes 'faster' as literal physical speed, which the author brackets. B is wrong because it overstates into a claim that people have given up understanding. D is wrong because it shifts to prediction of the future, whereas the phrase concerns making present experience meaningful.
